text: So what we're looking at is we're doing a robotic prostatectomy, and there's different ways to do it. This is the transperitoneal approach, and we're doing the case in a posterior fashion or through a posterior approach transperitoneally.

The camera view shows the interior of the peritoneal cavity. The surgeon points to the bladder with the right hand, highlighting a bulge caused by the balloon of the Foley catheter. The surgeon elevates the bladder and begins the posterior approach by incising the peritoneum beneath the bladder.

The assistant uses a sucker port to provide upwards traction while the surgeon applies downwards traction to mobilize the seminal vesicles and vasa during the posterior approach of the robotic prostatectomy.

In the posterior approach of the robotic prostatectomy, the surgeon has freed the tissues on the right side. The vasa deferens, a white tubular structure, is grasped with the left hand and cut, releasing yellowish fluid identified as semen. The surgeon then proceeds to free the left seminal vesicle, having already freed the right.

The surgeon uses scissors to make an incision into the non-VA's fascia, while the left hand holds the freed seminal vesicles. The right hand pushes the rectum away from the prostate, which is held up above, creating a clear separation between these structures.

The surgeon cauterizes the superficial dorsal vein using the left hand, then cuts and removes it to clear the surgical field. Excess fat above the prostate is removed to improve visibility. The prostate, with its distinctive walnut shape, is exposed, showing the shiny surface of the endopelvic fascia on both sides. The surgeon uses scissors to create a plane between the bladder and the prostate.
The surgeon carefully dissects to maintain a dry surgical field, identifying the bladder fibers forming a funnel shape. As the bladder neck is opened, the yellow Foley catheter becomes visible.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to latch onto the eyelet for traction, while the assistant pulls on the catheter from outside the body, elevating the prostate. This setup facilitates dissection on the posterior wall of the bladder, aiming to create a small bladder neck for easier anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies the pink circular bladder neck located below the Foley catheter and proceeds to peel and dissect the bladder away from the prostate. The anterior bladder has been separated, and the surgeon is now moving the posterior bladder away from the prostate.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to pull the seminal vesicles and vasa into view, with the semivascular structures visible in the center of the surgical field. The right seminal vesicle is clearly seen, and the surgeon prepares to elevate the left side.

The surgeon begins dissecting the pedicles and neurovascular bundles on the left side, focusing on the pedicle, which serves as the blood supply to the prostate. A permanent clip is applied to the pedicle to secure it without causing harm to the patient.

The surgeon places an additional clip on the pedicle to minimize back bleeding, with multiple clips used depending on the prostate size. The neurovascular bundle is gently dissected away from the surrounding vascular tissue to minimize trauma and avoid tearing into the prostate.

The surgeon completes dissection on the left side, revealing the shiny white surface of the prostate. The prostate is isolated to the dorsal vein complex, which is then tied off to minimize bleeding. The procedure is now moving to the right side of the prostate for similar dissection and isolation.

The surgeon ties a slip knot around the dorsal vein complex after dissecting the neurovascular bundles. A suture is placed around the dorsal vein complex, incorporating part of the urethra, and is brought up to the pubic symphysis. The suture is rolled one or two times to reconstruct the angle lost after cutting through the puboprostatic ligaments.

The surgeon performs anterior reconstruction and ties the dorsal vein complex, cutting it to expose the urethra. The dissection is kept close to the knot, ensuring no violation of the prostate. The neurovascular bundles are positioned laterally, allowing safe use of cautery with cooling irrigation fluid to prevent thermal spread. Extra cautery is applied to address any potential prostate cancer cells in the area.

The urethra is visible as a tubular structure, with longitudinal fibers coming into view as the prostate tissue is dissected away. The surgeon cuts into the urethra, exposing the Foley catheter, which appears as a long tube. The posterior part of the urethra is freed, with minimal bleeding in the surgical field. The surgeon cuts away recto-urethralis tissue, completing the dissection and freeing the prostate.

The surgeon places the prostate in a containment bag to prevent the spread of cancer cells. The anastomosis begins using a quill suture, with the surgeon treating the sutures like ice picks to grasp the posterior bladder and expose the mucosa. The assistant gently pulls down on the anterior bladder neck, allowing the mucosa to be visible. The surgeon pulls one needle through, followed by the other, bringing both out simultaneously.

The surgeon follows the catheter and twists to grasp the posterior urethra, using the arc of the needle to bring it out. The assistant pushes the catheter in again, and the surgeon repeats the steps, securing a six o'clock suture on both the bladder and urethra. The surgeon prepares to throw another suture on the bladder side before pulling the bladder and urethra together.

The surgeon gently tugs on the bladder and urethra until they are in contact, ensuring good apposition. Using a Van Veltoven suture technique, the surgeon stitches from six o'clock to twelve o'clock, with minimal slippage due to the quill suture.

The surgeon completes suturing on one side, bringing the suture through the urethra near the twelve o'clock position. The suture is released without needing additional tension, ensuring no gaps on the left side. The surgeon prepares to repeat the suturing steps on the right side.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis near the twelve o'clock position, using the robotic system to break the suture. One or two throws are made to secure the suture. The bladder is filled with fluid to test for leaks, and a drain is placed through a port site to monitor for bleeding or fluid leakage. The abdominal pressure is reduced to check for bleeding, and the robotic instruments are removed as the procedure concludes.
